fig01:
%
  Accuracy (\%) of four methods across five benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, MATH, GPQA).
  Error bars show sample standard deviation over 5 random seeds.
  \textbf{OursModel} (green, cross-hatch) achieves the highest accuracy on every benchmark,
  with an average gain of \textbf{+6.4\,pp} over the strongest baseline (Method-B).
  Data are synthetic but follow realistic distributions; see \texttt{data.json}.%
fig02: Validation loss over 50 training steps for three methods (mean $\pm$ 95\% CI across $n{=}5$ seeds, log-scale $y$-axis). \textbf{OursModel} converges fastest and achieves the lowest final loss, with a gap of $\Delta{=}0.35$ over Baseline at the final step.
fig03:
Chinchilla-style scaling law: validation loss vs.\ model size across 80 runs.
Each point is one model; the orange line is an OLS fit
($\hat{y} = -0.0449\,x + 0.6209$, $R^{2}=0.877$).
The starred red point (\textbf{OursModel}) is the closest data point to the target
coordinates $(\log_{10}\text{params}=9.449,\ \text{loss}=0.2096)$.
fig04:
\textbf{Method overview and empirical results.}
\textbf{(A)} The proposed 3-stage pipeline: an encoder with self-attention (Stage~1)
feeds a recurrent memory module (Stage~2) that supplies context to a skill-conditioned
decoder (Stage~3), with a residual skip connection from Stage~1 to Stage~3 and an
attention-feedback loop from Stage~2 to Stage~1.
\textbf{(B)} Ablation study: removing any single component (attention~$-$attn,
memory~$-$mem, residual~$-$residual, or skill decoder~$-$skill) degrades accuracy
relative to the full model (64.7\%), with the memory module contributing the largest
gain ($\Delta{=}9.6$ pp).
\textbf{(C)} Training-loss curves over 30k steps showing that our method converges
faster and to a lower final loss than the baseline.
\textbf{(D)} Sensitivity sweep across a $5{\times}5$ grid of hyperparameters
$\{S_0\text{--}S_4\}\times\{P_0\text{--}P_4\}$; scores in [0,\,1], higher is better.
